verdict.visual_pleasure: The serif stack, drop caps on chapter openers, accent-color blockquotes with warm tint, and a restrained color palette make this a genuinely pleasant read. The typography is confident and consistent across all 48 laws. The only drag is that the design note banner ('Lite mode — no Channel B illustrations, no Mermaid diagrams') reminds you up front that visual richness was deliberately withheld. Within those constraints the typographic execution is clean enough to earn a 4 — you would keep scrolling, but you would occasionally wish for a visual break.
verdict.navigation: This is the strongest dimension. A 48-item sticky TOC with per-law reading time estimates, scroll-spy highlighting of the current law with an accent underline, a live progress bar showing both a colored strip and a '% read' counter — everything works together. On a document of this length and density, being able to jump directly to any of the 48 laws without hunting is a real speed advantage. A reader who only wants the laws relevant to a current situation can use this as a reference without friction. Full marks.
verdict.comprehension: All ten comprehension questions are answerable directly from the HTML. The key narrative facts are preserved with high fidelity: D'Artagnan's arrest of Fouquet the day after the party; Colbert as the bland replacement; Bismarck's cabinet appointment weeks after the peace speech; the brick man drawing over a thousand visitors on day one before police stopped it due to traffic; Beauvallon shooting Dujarier after the party quarrel; Wu Chao smothering her newborn and the empress being executed for it; Brecht wearing a suit and deferring to the committee while the Hollywood 19 argued; the Strategy of the Crown with Columbus as an illustration; Alexander's complaint about Philip leaving nothing extraordinary to conquer; Mao's mercury-like dispersal in Manchuria and Sun-tzu's closing definition of genius as formlessness. Every source ID cited in the test maps to a passage in the document. Ten out of ten.
verdict.illustration: Lite mode is declared honestly in the design note, so this score is for what the visual structure actually delivers without images or diagrams. The anchor blockquotes with their three-part treatment — accent left border, warm tinted background, source-id attribution — do real work: they visually separate primary source material from the framing prose. The pull-quote and sidenote components are styled but never instantiated in the content, which is a missed opportunity. Without images, diagrams, or even deployed pull-quotes, the page is visually monotonous across 48 identical sections. Three is the honest score: the visual vocabulary exists and the blockquotes help, but the page could not be mistaken for illustrated.
verdict.books_finished_rate: At 83 estimated reading minutes for all 48 laws, this artifact achieves its primary purpose. The stories are the book's real content — Greene's arguments are memorable because of the anecdotes — and the key stories are here: Fouquet's party and arrest, Tesla and Edison, Wu Chao and the infant, Brecht at HUAC, Alexander vs. Philip, Mao's formless campaign. The framing sentences before each law give enough context to make the blockquotes land. The 8.4% coverage figure in the footer is honest but not alarming — the original is famously padded, and the compression ratio is appropriate. A reader who finishes this artifact can hold a conversation about the book, recall its key examples, and apply its principles. That is the functional test for skipping the original, and this passes it.

comprehension_answers.brief_answer: The next day, Fouquet was arrested by the king's head musketeer, D'Artagnan.
Louis XIV replaced Fouquet with the bland Colbert — preferable because less dazzling and less threatening.
A few weeks after the speech, the king made Bismarck a cabinet minister.
Over a thousand people entered the museum on day one; police ordered the stunt stopped days later because crowds blocked traffic.
Dujarier insulted critic Beauvallon at the party, a duel was called the next morning, and Beauvallon — one of the best pistol shots in France — shot and killed him.
Wu smothered her own newborn immediately after the empress visited; the empress was charged with murder and executed; Wu was crowned empress.
Brecht wore a suit, answered politely, and deferred — others argued and refused to answer. His reason: playing martyr would cost them years of work; outfoxing the committee was smarter.
The Strategy of the Crown; Columbus is cited as an illustration (Isabella granting his wishes).
Alexander marched immediately to suppress rebellions his own way; his complaint: 'My father will go on conquering till there is nothing extraordinary left for me to do.'

Communists dispersed in small elusive units, seizing and abandoning towns, moving like mercury. Sun-tzu is quoted defining genius as 'the ability to gain victory by changing and adapting according to the opponent.'
